coredata.dc:title: Introducing lignocellulose valorization into multifunctional water purification: Metal-ion sensing, antibiotic dissociation, and capacitive deionization
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jun
Zhou, Pengfei
Xiao, Xiao
Zhang, Chuntao
Huo, Kaifu
Xu, Jikun
coredata.dc:description: State-of-the art biomass valorization represents a classic yet fast-growing area in striding the threshold of sustainable energy-environmental research. How to explore a waste-free route is of great concern to maneuver lignocellulose potentials with multifunctional water purification. As a proof-of-concept, we proposed a tandem manufacture with the aid of acidic hydrothermal fractionation for the multi-scale wheat straw valorization into green-emitting carbon quantum dots (GCQDs), as well as, the Co, N co-doped biochar (Co-N-C). By means of xylan-based oligomers and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA), the GCQDs emit stable green luminescence in a broad excitation span, which can be adopted to selectively sense metal-ions (e.g., Ag+ and Fe3+) within an ultra-wide concentration. Through a facile confinement of Co-MOFs, the hierarchically porous Co-N-C with lamellar edge structure and redox active sites are achieved to exert high-performance catalytic antibiotic dissociation (an optimal rate of degrading ∼97 % tetracycline, TC) and capacitive deionization (CDI, salt adsorption capacities of 23.5 mg g−1 NaCl and 44.07 mg g−1 CdCl2) with excellent cycling stability. Taking advantages of theoretical models, radicals trapping and direct charge-transfer, a synergistic benefit from reactive oxygen species and interface electron shuttling is exerted to efficient antibiotic decomposition. The salt-ionic diffusion and capture mechanisms of Co-N-C are plotted via layered conductive framework, ion intercalation/electro-sorption, and faradaic redox conversion. We believe that the present work paves a new avenue to waste-free lignocellulose conversion that involves matching the sustainable water purification with maximum biorefining revenue and feasibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass valorization
Multifunctional water purification
Carbon quantum dots
Antibiotic dissociation
Capacitive deionization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent metal-ion sensing performance of carbon quantum dots
Structure of Co-N-C and its antibiotic decomposition performance
Capacitive deionization performance for water desalination

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic flowchart of the stepwise lignocellulose fractionation into green-emissive carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) and Co, N-codoped biochar (Co-N-C) that are capable of multifunctional water purification, including selective metal-ion sensing (Fe3+ and Ag+), antibiotic decomposition (tetracycline), and capacitive deionization (NaCl and CdCl2). (B) TEM, lateral size distribution, HRTEM, HAADF-STEM and the corresponding EDS mapping images of GCQDs. (C) AFM image, (D) Raman spectrum, and (E) full XPS survey spectrum of GCDs. High-resolution XPS of (F) C 1s, (G) O 1s and (H) N 1s for GCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optical and sensing properties of GCQDs. (A) The ultraviolet–visible absorption, excitation (Ex) and emission spectra (Em) of GCQDs (insert picture indicates the color under ultraviolet light). (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of GCQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Normalized PL spectra of excitation-dependent behavior. (D) Normalized 3D-mapping FL spectra of GCQDs. (E) Lifetime decay spectra. (F) The relative fluorescence emission intensities upon adding various kinds of metal-ions. (G) Fluorescent spectra upon adding Fe3+ concentrations of 0–2000 μM. Linear fitting plot of (F0 − F)/F0 vs. Fe3+ concentration of (H) 0–2000 and (I) 100–900 μM. (J) Fluorescent spectra upon adding Ag+ concentrations of 0–2000 μM. (K) Linear fitting plot of (F0 − F)/F0 vs. Ag+ concentration of 200–2000 μM. (L) Schematic diagram showing the potential fluorescent quenching mechanism with the addition of metal-ions (Fe3+ and Ag+).

Identifying the morphologies and structures of Co-N-C via calcinating the MOFs-confined lignocellulose. (A) TEM, HRTEM, and (B) HAADF-STEM and the corresponding element mapping images. (C) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (insert depicts the distributed pore volume). (D) Full XPS survey. (E) Raman spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectra of (F) C 1s, (G) Co 2p and (H) N 1s.

Performance of antibiotic decomposition. (A) Electrostatic potential (ESP) distributions of tetracycline (TC) and peroxydisulfate (PDS)-(NH4)2S2O8. (B) The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energies of TC and PDS. Degradation efficiencies and the corresponding reaction kinetics (k, min−1) of the tetracycline (TC) solution at room temperature by varying (C and D) the catalyst (Co-N-C) amount, (E and F) TC concentration, and (G and H) initial pH value.

Identification of antibiotic degradation mechanisms. Quenching experiments against reactive oxygen species via gradient (A) ethanol (EtOH), (B) tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), (C) p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and (D) furfuryl alcohol (FFA) as scavengers for detecting SO4
¯/
OH,
OH, O2
¯, and 1O2, respectively. Comparison of the (E) inhibition efficiency and (F) calculated rate constant k in the presence of diverse scavengers at large concentrations. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra for capturing (G) SO4
¯/
OH and (H) O2
¯ in the presence of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), as well as (I) 1O2 in the presence of 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP) as a spin-trapping agent. The changes in (J) open-circuit potential and (K) linear sweep voltammetry (LSV, I–t curve) tests with the presence of Co-N-C upon adding the PDS and TC. Proposed mechanism behind the Co-N-C catalytic TC decomposition involving three pathways of radical generation (SO4
¯/
OH,
OH and O2
¯), singlet oxygen (1O2), and direct surface electron transmission.

Capacitive deionization (CDI) performance. (A) CV curves of Co-N-C in 1 M NaCl solution under different scan rates. (B) The galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves at different current densities. (C) Nyquist plot with an inset showing the equivalent circuit. (D) Specific capacitance of Co-N-C in salt solution versus scan rate. (E) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) profile representing the capacitive contribution to charge storage at a scan rate of 20 mV/s. (F) Normalized ratio of the capacitive to diffusion contributions at a varying scan rate of 5 to 100 mV/s. (G) Salt adsorption capacity (SAC) of NaCl solution. (H) SAC of CdCl2 solution. (I) Comparison of SAC results between NaCl and CdCl2 solution. (J) CDI cycling stability after electrochemical adsorption–desorption. (K) SAC results at different operating voltages. (L) CDI Ragone plots of correlating SAC and mean salt adsorption rate (MSAR).

head.ce:title: Introducing lignocellulose valorization into multifunctional water purification: Metal-ion sensing, antibiotic dissociation, and capacitive deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Sichuan 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biomass Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Sichuan 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: State-of-the art biomass valorization represents a classic yet fast-growing area in striding the threshold of sustainable energy-environmental research. How to explore a waste-free route is of great concern to maneuver lignocellulose potentials with multifunctional water purification. As a proof-of-concept, we proposed a tandem manufacture with the aid of acidic hydrothermal fractionation for the multi-scale wheat straw valorization into green-emitting carbon quantum dots (GCQDs), as well as, the Co, N co-doped biochar (Co-N-C). By means of xylan-based oligomers and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA), the GCQDs emit stable green luminescence in a broad excitation span, which can be adopted to selectively sense metal-ions (e.g., Ag+ and Fe3+) within an ultra-wide concentration. Through a facile confinement of Co-MOFs, the hierarchically porous Co-N-C with lamellar edge structure and redox active sites are achieved to exert high-performance catalytic antibiotic dissociation (an optimal rate of degrading ∼97 % tetracycline, TC) and capacitive deionization (CDI, salt adsorption capacities of 23.5 mg g−1 NaCl and 44.07 mg g−1 CdCl2) with excellent cycling stability. Taking advantages of theoretical models, radicals trapping and direct charge-transfer, a synergistic benefit from reactive oxygen species and interface electron shuttling is exerted to efficient antibiotic decomposition. The salt-ionic diffusion and capture mechanisms of Co-N-C are plotted via layered conductive framework, ion intercalation/electro-sorption, and faradaic redox conversion. We believe that the present work paves a new avenue to waste-free lignocellulose conversion that involves matching the sustainable water purification with maximum biorefining revenue and feasibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A dilemma of negative environmental impact and exhausted crude petroleum ignited by the large-scale consuming fuel oil has led to a pressing demand in the advancement of renewable energy, materials and water purification [1,2]. Biomass is an abundant, easily available, and low-cost resource that exhibits unique carbon–neutral properties, rendering it a sustainable candidate to water and energy applications [3,4]. Thereinto, harnessing the individual potentials of lignocellulose constituents (i.e., cellulose, lignin and hemicelluloses) has become an indispensable yet challenging part of biomass valorization [5,6]. It is intractable to efficiently convert three building blocks into versatile value-added items due to the firmly compact plant cell walls with robust cross-linked structure, building an inherent recalcitrance to fully exploiting and/or modifying lignocelluloses [7]. Developing a low-cost and eco-friendly approach to dissociate plant cell wall with pursuing waste-free utilization is an ultimate aspiration of lignocellulose biorefining [8,9]. Over the past decades, the vast majority of biomass engineering have relied heavily on cellulose within the dull industrial purposes such as papermaking and bioethanol products, which has led to a substantial water pollution associated with the insufficient utilization of available resources [10,11].

With the advance of chemical engineering, the emphasis of current pretreatment technologies is generally placed on the waste-free lignocellulose biorefining [12,13]. Recently, a novel coupling method of liquid-hot-water and low-melting-point deep eutectic solvent pretreatment is developed to fabricate three high-value potential items, including xylan-derived activated nanocarbon, lignin nanospheres, and lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers [14]. Sun et al. successfully explored a two-step processing of birchwood that produced the bio-based surfactants from xylan and lignin accounting for 29.0 % of the total mass, thereby substantially enhancing the compatibility of biomass with water [15]. A pretreatment approach that combines a water-soluble p-toluenesulfonic acid and pentanol was conducted to isolate the purified lignin, whilst generating bioethanol and xylic acid at the same time [16]. As mentioned before, the contamination and scarcity of drinkable water have seriously hampered the renovation of biomass industries. Access to available clean water has been a grand challenge worldwide that is exacerbated annually with the symbols of increased pollution, population growth, and climate change. As a natural purifier of atmospheric water, lignocellulose is highly desirable to prepare water remediation materials that is an eco-feasible scheme with a low-carbon footprint, alleviating humanity from the predicament with sustainable biomass-water-energy nexus [17]. It thus is meaningful to leverage their immanent advantages by encompassing water purification functions with a perspective of achieving waste-free biorefining. Hence the integrated approaches within biomass valorization are imperative to address sanitary water challenges, and the material innovation has personated a crucial role in water treatment such as sensing metal-ions, removing pharmaceutical antibiotic, and water desalination.

Although various bio-based materials are manufactured from lignocellulosic chemistry [18,19], the potential relationship between building blocks and water purification is universally overlooked. Herein, we proposed a novel scenario of lignocellulose valorization coupling with multifunctional water purification (e.g., selective metal-ion sensing, antibiotic dissociation, and electrochemical deionization) via the successive fractionation of wheat straw. In brief, an initially acid-catalytic pretreatment with the presence of m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) is recommended to acquire the bright green-luminescent carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) via a two-step hydrothermal approach, which can be used to the selectively identify Ag+ and Fe3+ ions in water. Following the first step of hydrothermal treatment, the confinement of cobalt-based metal–organic-frameworks (Co-MOFs) into residual straw is facilitate to fabricate reactive Co, N-coordinated biochar (Co-N-C) via a facile pyrolysis processing, which not only efficiently activates persulfate to decompose antibiotics (tetracycline, TC) but also functions as an electrode in capacitive deionization (CDI) cell for water desalination. The superiority of the present work is to stride the threshold of cascade lignocellulose valorization with the ability of multifunctional water purification, which drives the orthodox biomass industries to serve environmental engineering. We thus believe that this platform ignites a dazzling spark from waste-free biomass valorization that also seek its suitable orientation in the domains of sustainable low-carbon materials and wastewater remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent metal-ion sensing performance of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of upcycling renewable lignocellulose fundamentally depends on the task-specific deconstruction that releases their unique potentials within the framework of carbon neutrality [20,21]. Due to the presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl and aldehyde), the branched hemicelluloses are prone to separated and/or hydrolyzed in a moderate environment [22]. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, we proposed a cascade route to convert wheat straw with the assistance of hydrothermal xylan dissociation catalyzed by the organic acid m-PDA. During the acidic pretreatment, the amino groups from m-PDA interact with water by accepting hydronium ions, which in turn create an acidic media to accelerate xylan hydrolysis into the liquid phase [23]. Owing to the superiority in the nucleation of carbon structures, the xylan-derived oligomers are of importance to the controllable synthesis of light-emitting CQDs in a sustainable manner [24–26]. Adopting a moderate organic acid not only introduces surface functionality into the structure of CQDs with alleviating the excessive carbonization, but also passivates their interface and/or edge with enhanced optical performance. More importantly, the hydrolytic xylan degradation not only creates a hierarchical porous, loosen structure of straw but also introduces the heteroatom species, which greatly facilitates its subsequent conversion and interfacial functionality. The crude fiber of wheat straw exhibits a highly intact and compact structure (Fig. S1A). Following acidic hydrothermal treatment, the exterior layer of wheat straw undergoes an obvious peeling process (Fig. S1B), exposing a high level of cracked architecture and well-aligned channels (Fig. S1C) that forms a suitable substance to loading Co-MOFs (Fig. S1D and E). Afterwards, the GCQDs and Co-N-C materials can be easily prepared via diverse thermal treatments to achieve multifunctional water purification.

As the state-of-the-art zero-dimensional (0D) materials in the family of nanocarbons, the CQDs of <10 nm are commonly synthesized from biomass via a facile hydrothermal method [27,28]. As depicted by TEM image in Fig. 1B, the GCQDs present a standard quasi-nanospheric shape with an average size of 1.97 ± 0.59 nm. A lattice d-spacing of ∼0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal lattice in graphene demonstrates that the as-prepared GCQDs exhibit a lamellar graphene-like structure [29]. Benefitting from the existing m-PDA, the successful introduction of N-dopants into GCQDs is confirmed by the uniform elemental distribution mapping (Fig. 1B). The AFM image in Fig. 1C also affirms the evenly dispersed state of individual GCQDs nanoparticles. In the Raman spectrum of Fig. 1D, the relative level of disorder and graphitic structures (I D/I G = 0.94) represent a direct evidence to a highly graphitized GCQDs with functionalized surface defects, signifying the potential ability of m-PDA-assisted hydrothermal process to regulate the degree of graphitization in their carbon core. Meanwhile, the amino groups attached on m-PDA are able to confer N-doping characteristics on GCQDs during hydrothermal procedure [30], in which the hemicelluloses with a quantity of –COOH and –OH can react with the amino groups to firmly binding N species. In addition, the incorporation of heteroatoms has been frequently adopted to enhance the luminescent properties of CQDs through manipulating their defect architectures and surface charges [31]. As illustrated in Fig. 1E, the full-survey XPS spectrum of GCQDs reveals the explicit locations of C, O, and N at approximately 285 eV, 533 eV, and 399 eV respectively. The high-resolution C1s profile can be fitted into three peaks (Fig. 1F), assigning to the C−C/CC (∼284.5 eV), C−O/C−N (∼286.5 eV), and CO (∼288.5 eV) [32]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) contains the essential bands corresponding to C−O (∼533 eV) and CO (∼531.5 eV) [33]. As depicted in Fig. 1H, the N1s spectrum can be easily split into N−H (∼399 eV, pyridinic N) and C−N (∼400.5 eV, graphitic N) [34]. The XPS spectra indicate the graphitized carbon nuclei of GCQDs with N, O-containing active sites that are originated from the m-PDA-hydrolyzed hemicellulose sugars [29].

From Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis spectrum of GCQDs exhibits a broad wavelength UV absorption ranging from 200 to 500 nm. The absorption peak observed at 250–300 nm reveals the π–π* transition that is linked to the CC/CN linkages. The presence of absorption peak in the range of 320–500 nm might be attributed to n–π* transition featuring the CO/CN bonded structure [35]. In the insert of Fig. 2A, the GCQDs emit a bright green fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The fluorescent spectra at various excitation wavelengths are displayed in Fig. 2B, reaching a maximum emission wavelength of 523 nm. In specific, the normalized excitation-dependent PL spectra exhibit a weak red-shifted behavior with the increase of excitation wavelength from 370 to 470 nm (Fig. 2C), which has particularly close ties to the surface defects of GCQDs. It is witnessed that this faint red-shift of emission wavelength (515–523 nm) endows the as-prepared GCQDs with a remarkable ability to resist excitation-interfering luminescence at the amplified condition (370–470 nm). The normalized 3D-mapping photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of GCQDs illustrates a clear fluorescence property with a highly concentrated emission region upon the extensional excitation intervals (Fig. 2D). The PL decay kinetics of GCQDs is confirmed via fitting the lifetime spectrum of GCQDs at the optimum excitation wavelength (Fig. 2E), which presents an average lifetime of 7.12 ns including the fast and low components with the lifetime of 1.13 and 5.83 ns respectively. These responses refer to switching the excited-electrons deactivation to ground state through radiative and non-radiative pathways [35].

Benefitting from visualizing the PL quenching results of several metal-ions upon GCQDs, the selective sensing abilities of Ag+ and Fe3+ are observed under UV-light with an extreme quenching phenomenon (Fig. 2F).Clearly, Fig. 2G–J illustrate the direct influence of varying Ag+ and Fe3+ concentrations on the normalized PL intensities of GCQDs respectively, wherein the increased concentrations of metal-ions (0–2000 μM) transmit a weakened PL intensities. Ranging the Ag+ concentration from 0 to 2000 μM, an acceptable linear correlation is achieved with a coefficient R 2 of 0.84829 (Fig. 2H). At a low concentration of 800 μM, the linear response is highly fitted via a coefficient R 2 of 0.93679 (Fig. 2I), indicating that the as-prepared GCQDs are more sensitive to sensing the low concentration of Ag+. Despite the fact that the linear response relationship weakens at a lower Fe3+ concentrations of <200 μM, a superior linear response coefficient (R 2 = 0.98949) within a wide range of 200–2000 μM are obtained to verify the strong chelating ability of Fe3+ with alterable valences (Fig. 2K). It thus is believed that the functional species of CQDs (e.g., −OH, −COOH and –NH2) are of importance to form ionic complexation via weak interactions (metal-coordination, π-cation bond, and electrostatic attraction), referring to the selective PL quenching of metal-ions with specific valences and/or surface charges (Fig. 2L) [36]. The tunable interfacial decoration of CQDs that holds a huge potential to selectively recognize metal-ions in water with a unique responding ability is imperative for expanding biomass-based CQDs into practical environmental application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure of Co-N-C and its antibiotic decomposition performance
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past several decades, deriving multifunctional porous biochar from lignocelluloses with low-carbon emission has been widely emphasized in the scientific field of wastewater treatment, such as pollutant adsorption, catalytic antibiotic decomposition, etc. [37]. The naturally hierarchical well-aligned porosity of lignocellulose is of great concern to facilitate efficient mass transfer (e.g., nutrients, water and ions). The numerous oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., −OH and –COOH) with high reactivity also expand the potential advantages in the surface modification of biochar [38].The acidic hydrothermal pretreatment of wheat straw is conducive to expose the –OH and –COOH sites as the suitable carrier to binding with Co-MOFs and preventing aggregation (Fig. S1D and E). Moreover, the porous structure of wheat straw can effectively confine the growth, size and distribution of the nano-scale Co-MOFs that are easily to form clumps/agglomerates [39]. From Fig. 3 A and B, the Co-MOFs surface are confined in the microchannels with a layered carbon structure, wherein the oxygen-rich functional species providing the coordination with Co-ions improves the excellent dispersion of C, N, O and Co elements. As illustrated in XRD spectrum of Fig. S1F, the peaks at ∼45° and 50° correspond to the 111, 220 crystal plane of Co, respectively [40], suggesting that the format of Co-N-C biochar belongs to a Co, N-doped graphite carbon with hierarchical porous structure [39]. Due to the synergistic benefits from synthesized Co-MOFs and natural straw, the as-prepared Co-N-C material exhibits an ultra-high total specific surface area of ∼1713.3 m2 g−1 and hierarchical porous structure with micro-, meso-, and macro-pores (Fig. 3C). An average total pore volume of 0.89 cm3 g−1 is calculated, of which the microporous pore volume contributes 57.87 % (0.514 cm3 g−1). The ideally hierarchical pores of Co-N-C favor the rapid adsorption and penetration of organic molecules and/or salt ions, thus speeding up the subsequent mass transfer and reaction kinetics [41]. With the aid of confinement growth of Co-MOFs in microchannels, the formation of Co-N-C is also confirmed by the specific peaks of Co 2p (∼782 eV) and N 1s (∼399 eV) in the full-survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3D). As a critical process, pyrolysis not only increases the graphite content of Co-N-C to improve conductivity, but also reinforces the strong metal-support interfacial interactions [42]. As can be seen from Fig. 3E, the Raman spectrum reveals that the value of I D/I G is 1.02, which also indicates that the defect structures of Co-N-C are introduced into a highly conductive graphite carbon matrix under a high-temperature pyrolysis. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3F) presents four distinct peaks, corresponding to various species of C−C/CC, C−O/C−N, CO, and π-π* [43]. As illustrated in Fig. 3G, the high-resolution Co 2p pattern is resolved into the distinct signals of Co–O (∼782 and 796 eV), Co–N x coordination (∼783 and 798 eV) and satellite peak [40]. The deconvolution of high-resolution N 1s spectrum is composed of three peaks that are ascribed to pyridine N, pyrrolic N, and graphite N in the fitting range of 398–402 eV with the successful coordination of Co–N x sites (Fig. 3H) [40], facilitating the defects formation, surface charge transfer, and the subsequent adsorption of TC molecules and salt ions.

It is witnessed that the human society is trying to weasel out of the awful water predicament encompassing the excessive discharge of pharmaceutical antibiotics into freshwater [3]. Low-cost biomass-derived carbons with highly-dispersed reactive metallic sites have shown great potentials in advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) due to the superior catalytic activity, selectivity and stability [44]. Hence the catalytic efficiencies of TC decomposition is evaluated via the as-prepared Co-N-C activating peroxodisulfate (PDS) system, mainly including the influences of catalyst amount, PDS dosage, TC concentration, and working pH. Fig. 4 A demonstrates the surface electrostatic potential distribution of TC and PDS molecules, wherein the blue and red areas represent the electron-rich and electron-deficient parts that are easily attacked by free radicals to trigger the chemical bond breakage in these groups [45]. Moreover, the energy ladder diagrams of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TC and PDS molecules are determined in Fig. 4B. A lower HOMO energy of TC (−1.09 eV) with the narrow band-gap (6.53 eV) as compared with PDS (1.63 eV with gap of 15.51 eV) is conducive to escape electrons, which will be susceptible to electrophilic attacks induced by the SO4
− and OH free-radicals [46]. Upon the gradual increase of catalyst from 2.5 to 7.5 mg, the TC removal efficiency is improved from 69.7 % to 98.4 % within 30 min (Fig. 4C). The corresponding reaction kinetic constants (k, min−1) is calculated to further verify the intensively catalytic performance (Fig. 4D). It is noteworthy that the TC removal capacity of ∼98 % is achieved with an optimum amount of 5 mg, suggesting the quasi-saturation status of Co-N-C active sites. An optimal PDS dosage of 0.5 mM is also determined by the degradation efficiencies (Fig. S2A) and the corresponding k values (Fig. S2B). The elimination of high-concentration antibiotics that impede mass transfer and disrupt environmental pH has posed a pressing challenge of reduced removal performance [47]. Even at a TC concentration of 40 ppm, the degradation efficiency of Co-N-C is still above 85 % (Fig. 4E) with an acceptable k value of ∼0.05 min−1 (Fig. 4F). The retention of k value is maintained with 69.86 % as compared with that at a TC concentration of 20 ppm. More importantly, the environmental pH is also a critical factor that can significantly influence the performance of catalysts. As depicted in Fig. 4G, the stable catalytic activity of Co-N-C is observed within a wide pH range of 3–9 featuring the k values over ∼0.64 to 0.88 (Fig. 4H). In an ultrahigh alkaline condition (pH = 11), the reaction kinetics constant suddenly decreased to 0.0155 (Fig. 4H), whereas the removal rate of 58.3 % is still achieved. In Fig. S3A, the successive cycling durability of Co-N-C catalyst illustrates a ∼80 % retention of total TC dissociation efficiencies even after five repeated runs. From Fig. S3B, a reduced removal rate could be triggered by the slight Co leaching from the 1st to 5th cycling (0.0556 vs. 0.0042 mg L−1), as well as, the gradual accumulation of TC and/or its intermediates in the pores of the biochar substrate. It thus is evident that the Co-N-C catalyst has a promising practical treatment of antibiotic wastewater with a stable activation ability.

Facile free-radical quenching approaches are employed to identify the primary reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the Co-N-C/PDS system. Four representative scavengers, including ethanol (EtOH), tertbutyl alcohol (TBA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) and furfuryl alcohol (FFA), are selected to quench SO4
−/OH, OH, O2
− and 1O2 respectively [40]. As shown in Fig. 5 A, an insignificant impact of increasing EtOH concentration is observed on the TC dissociation, suggesting that the SO4
− radical plays a negligible role in this process. When the concentration reaches 2000 mM, the TC removal rate decreases to 71.5 % (Fig. 5B), indicating that the OH radical has a weak ability to decompose TC. The degradation reaction is highly suppressed using the low concentrations of p-BQ (20 mM) and FFA (60 mM), implying that the O2
− and 1O2 radicals both contribute significantly to the TC removal. A direct comparison of removal efficiencies (Fig. 5E) and reaction kinetic (Fig. 5F) between the various quenching tests suggests that the contribution of ROS is determined as the following sequence: O2
− > 1O2 > OH > SO4
−. As illustrated in Fig. 5G-I, the in-situ electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique is conducted to directly validate the generation of ROS. By using specific reagents of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP), the distinct signals of OH/SO4
−, O2
− and 1O2 are trapped with the diversified intensities [46], which also demonstrates that the discrepant reactivities in four types of ROS accompanying by the quenching experiments.

On the other side, the existing studies have witnessed that TC degradation is likely to be realized through a surface electron shutting process in the catalyst/PDS complex [40,43,46]. It is imperative to determine the non-radical electron transfer reaction pathway through electrochemical measurements. As a proof-of-concept, the electron transmits between the interfaces of catalyst, TC and PDS is determined by detecting the open-circuit potential (Fig. 5J) and current changes (Fig. 5J) of the glassy carbon electrode coated with Co-N-C catalyst. Upon the addition of PDS, the potential increased suddenly from 0.2473 to 0.8363 V, which then decreased after the introduction of TC. This finding reveals that a high-potential active PDS complex can be generated on the Co-N-C surfaces. The subsequent addition of TC with a reduced redox potential provokes a redox reaction to decompose the PDS complex, thereby leading to an obvious decrease in the potential [43,46]. The similar current trend also confirms that as-prepared Co-N-C exhibits a considerable capacity to degrade TC through non-radical electron transfer route. As summarized in Fig. 5L, the PDS activation mechanism using the Co-N-C catalyst is presented to dissociate TC in a synergistic manner of free-radicals, singlet oxygen and surface electron shutting, which sheds the substantial lights on the functionality of lignocellulose-based environmental materials enabling homogeneous Fenton-like water treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacitive deionization performance for water desalination
ce:section.ce:para: To tackle the water scarcity issue, an ever-growing interest in the development of water desalination to purify seawater and sewage into freshwater is strongly motivated for human consumption, industries and agroforestry [48]. The cost-effective capacitive deionization (CDI), featuring electrical double layers (EDLs) principle at the electrode/water interface, are emerging as a promising brine desalination technology to generate a freshwater stream [49]. Metallic coordinated carbons are deemed as the potential anode materials with a bright future in CDI devices due to their high theoretical capacity and efficient selectivity to Cl− ions, in which the hierarchical porous Co-N-C delivers an excellent hybrid capacitive ion storage performances that have recently emerged as a suitable choice for CDI electrodes [50–52]. To estimate the fundamental capacitance and conductivity properties, the single-electrode performance of as-prepared Co-N-C is plotted in 6 M KOH electrolyte (Fig. S4). Benefitting from the electroactive redox sites and low electric resistance, the highly-conductive Co-N-C electrode delivers a superior specific capacitance of 936.6 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A/g and excellent rate performance (534 F g−1 even at 10 A/g). Owing to the practical paradigm of CDI placing in a highly concentrated saline, hence it is essential to investigate the electrochemical properties of Co-N-C in salt electrolyte. Fig. 6 A illustrates the CV curves of Co-N-C electrode at varied sweep rates across the potential range of −1 to 0 V, wherein a rectangular-like pattern suggests the main contribution of the EDLs capacitance behavior in the 1 M NaCl electrolyte. The CV profile maintains a rectangular shape even at a high scanning rate of 100 mV/s, implying its exceptional electrochemical stability. This excellent conductivity and polarization resistance is primarily derived from the carbon skeleton of biochar [53]. The constant galvanostatic current charge–discharge (GCD) curve displays the gradually prolonged triangular isosceles contour with the increase of current densities from 1 to 10 A/g (Fig. 6B), signifying the main double-layer capacitance characteristic of Co-N-C electrode. The impedance properties of Co-N-C possess a pivotal role in influencing the desalination procedure. As illustrated in Fig. 6C, the ultra-low internal resistance of ∼1 Ω in the high-frequency domain and the double layer diffusive behavior in the low-frequency region indicate the hierarchical porous Co-N-C with high conductivities, which can promote rapid diffusion of ions and electrons. Due to the differential electrolytes, the specific capacitance of 95.3 F g−1 is achieved at an initial current density of 1 A/g (Fig. 6D). Even at a high current density of 10 A/g, the specific capacitance still retains at 52 F g−1 with a retention of ∼55 %. The electrochemical behavior is investigated to split the processes in terms of capacitance- and diffusion-controlled functions. At a scan rate of 20 mV/s, the contributions of capacitance- (49.5 %) and diffusion-controlled (50.5 %) processes are achieved a dynamic balance of as-prepared Co-N-C electrode (Fig. 6E). With the acceleration of sweep rate (5 to 100 mV/s), the capacitance-controlled contribution of gradually increases from 36 to 74 % (Fig. 6F). It is evident that the as-fabricated Co-N-C features a substantial capacitance-controlled behavior with an assignable faradaic conversion, revealing the redox reactive active sites are also beneficial to improve the entire CDI desalination performance.

To further investigate the specific CDI performance of Co-N-C, the desalination experiments are carried out using metal salt solutions of NaCl and CdCl2 [54]. As depicted in Fig. S5A of the CDI setup, the cell architecture consists of current collectors and porous Co-N-C electrodes as adsorption media. It can be separated by an open channel and pumping feed water flow parallel in direction to the assembled electrodes alignment. In addition, the water contact angle of ∼112° endows Co-N-C with a weak hydrophobic ability that is facilitate to the spacing competition and/or gambling between water molecules and salt ions (Fig. S5B). As the salt concentration increases from 100 to 1000 mg L−1, the enhanced salt adsorption capacity (SAC) of NaCl from 9.05 to 23.5 mg g−1 over 30 min (Fig. 6G). Furthermore, the maximum SAC value of CdCl2 is determined as high as 44.07 mg g−1 at a concentration of 500 mg L−1 (Fig. 6H). It is emphasized that the SAC value is tend to an obvious decrease at a high CdCl2 concentration of 1000 mg L−1, which might be attributed to the saturation of adsorption sites enabling a rapid desorption. Fig. 6I presents a direct comparison between the electro-adsorption performances of two metallic salt solutions. The SAC value of divalent Cd2+ is higher than that of monovalent Na+, which can be ascribed to the high electrostatic attraction and redox capacity between Cd2+ and Co-N-C electrodes, leading to a faster transport of Cd2+ into the porous structure with the aid of electro-active sites [55]. In parallel, the reversible cycling and anti-interference performances are of importance to CDI desalination. As plotted in Fig. 6J, an excellent cycling durability of the Co-N-C is guaranteed by the retention of deionization efficiencies over 82.69 % after three constant adsorption/desorption loops. As can be seen from Fig. 6K, the change in external potentials also functions a crucial role in determining electrochemical adsorption [56], confirming that the SAC value of NaCl increases with the amplification of voltage. The SAC value still reaches 9.05 mg g−1 even at a low NaCl concentration (100 mg L−1) when the voltage increases to 1.2 V. The Ragone plots of SAC associated with the mean salt adsorption rate (MSAR) of the salt systems (i.e., NaCl and Cd2Cl) are fitted to further disclose a higher selectivity and adsorption rate of the as-fabricated Co-N-C for Cd2+ ions (Fig. 6L). To better understand the potential deionization mechanisms, the schematic of CDI process are illustrated in Fig. S5C. The CDI system is circulated to allow the rapid removal of charged ion species (e.g., Na+, Cd2+ and Cl−) in water via electrostatic or electrochemical interactions from conductive framework, ion intercalation, and faradaic redox conversion. Using a voltage across the CDI system, an electric field is generated between the electrodes due to potential difference. As a result, the ions temporarily immobilizes between the counter electrodes and then electrostatic storage is easily taken place at the interfaces including the stern and diffuse layers. Ions are strongly attracted to the surface of electrode by electrostatic forces in the stern layer, whereas the ions are still in the electrode vicinity due to the coulombic interactions with the stern layer [48]. With regard to a fresh cycle of water desalination, the absorbed ions can be flushed out via either short circuit or polarity reversal to regenerate the electrode, resulting in the release of the implanted ions back into the bulk liquid. By the multiscale deconstruction and functionality of wasted lignocellulose, the current research provides a comprehensive and feasible paradigm to achieving multifunctional sustainable water purification with the perspective of carbon–neutral development, including but not limited to metal-ions sensing, antibiotic decomposition and water desalination.

Bridging the gap between renewable lignocellulose valorization and multifunctional water purification is crucial yet still challenging to achieve sustainable low-carbon development with a perspective of waste-treats-pollutant. At this point, we developed a m-PDA-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment to deconstruct wheat straw into heteroatom-doped luminescent GCQDs capable of selectively fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ and Ag+ ions with acceptable sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD, 2.4–5.1 μM). In parallel, the highly conductive, porous, reactive Co-N-C biochar featuring a layered edge are equipped with customized functionality via confined Co-MOFs growth and pyrolysis to synchronously operate catalytic TC removal and electrochemical water desalination. The as-prepared Co-N-C demonstrates that a removal performance of TC with low HOMO energy and narrow bandgap are realized as high as 97 % by activating PDS, with the assistance of free radicals (OH, SO4
− and O2
−), non-radical 1O2 and surface electron shuttling. The stable catalytic activity of Co-N-C is observed within a wide pH range of 3–9, as well as, a high TC concentration of 40 ppm. It was fortunate that integrating uniform micropores of Co-MOFs and abundant meso-/macro-pores of natural straw is facilitate to construct a hierarchical porous network of Co-N-C (a high BET surface area of ∼1713.34 m2 g−1), bestowing it with the exceptional attributes of storing ions as a CDI electrode (the superior SAC values of 23.5 and 44.07 mg g−1 for NaCl and CdCl2 respectively). After three constant adsorption/desorption loops, an excellent cycling durability of as-fabricated Co-N-C is guaranteed by the retention of deionization efficiencies over 82.69 % in CDI system. This work provides a promising blueprint of driving multi-scale lignocellulose valorization into multifaceted water purification while also advancing the global vision of carbon neutrality.

Jun Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Pengfei Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiao Xiao: Resources, Data curation. Chuntao Zhang: Resources, Investigation. Kaifu Huo: Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Jikun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 22008159), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project (2022020801020312).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.154490.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
